intro.overview: This is your Unit 3 Engagement Packet for Module 4, and it works differently from the others: it’s an internal comparative engagement. NEXUS has password assessments running for two clients — Brazos Financial Group (Windows/NTLM) and Alamo Industries (Linux/SHA-512) — and you’re the analyst on both. You’ll compile one internal packet comparing the two platforms. It comes to your senior analyst, who repackages the relevant pieces into each client’s own deliverable. You are not addressing this report to a client.
objectives: Compile a professional security assessment report using work from Labs 4.1 and 4.2
Compare Windows NTLM and Linux SHA-512 password security architectures
Write an executive summary for non-technical stakeholders
Document professional reflection on the engagement

clientContext.description: This engagement spans two NEXUS clients: Brazos Financial Group (Dallas wealth management, SEC examination pending) and Alamo Industries (San Antonio aerospace/defense, CMMC Level 2 in progress). You assess both platforms in one internal packet. Your senior analyst (Maya Rodriguez) pulls from your work to build each client’s separate, confidential deliverable — the two clients never see each other’s results.

contacts.name: Ray Jimenez
contacts.title: CISO, Brazos Financial Group
contacts.quote: The SEC examiners are going to ask how we handle privileged access. I need an answer that holds up.

contacts.name: Robert "Bobby" Alamo III
contacts.title: CEO, Alamo Industries
contacts.quote: We can’t afford a production-floor incident. Show me where the credential gaps are and what fixing them costs.

callouts.title: Required: Use the Engagement Packet Template
callouts.text:
Don’t build this from a blank page. Your packet is internal source material — your senior analyst repackages it into each client’s report. Download the pre-formatted NEXUS template (cover, section headings, and auto-generating Table of Contents already set up): EP_M4_Password_Audit.docx. Full instructions are on the Engagement Packet Guide.

Submit as .docx (Microsoft Word) only — PDF submissions will not be accepted. Use Word’s built-in features for Table of Contents, page numbers, and citations.

callouts.title: Minimum Requirements
callouts.body: Your Engagement Packet must include: Part I (Timesheet, Runbook, Analyst Reflection) and Part II (Cover, Executive Summary, Methodology, Findings, Risk Analysis, Recommendations, Appendix), 20+ screenshots embedded across your Foundation Labs, VM hostname (kali-<your abc123>) visible in every terminal screenshot, and a minimum of 10 references. RFCs, NIST documents, CVE entries, and man pages all count. Your textbook does NOT count — use it to find primary sources, then cite those. See the Citations page.

callouts.title: Deadline Tip
callouts.text: Submit at least 10 minutes before the deadline, even if it’s not perfect. Submissions are unlimited — you can keep uploading better versions until time runs out. A 70 beats a zero every time.

mentorQuote.title: Founder & Principal, NEXUS Security
mentorQuote.quote: This report goes to two very different clients with very different concerns. Ray Jimenez at Brazos needs data to convince his board that current policies are inadequate. Bobby Alamo III needs CMMC evidence for his DoD contracts. Same technical work, different audiences. Your executive summary has to work for both.

youAlreadyHave.intro: This is NOT starting from scratch. You’ve been building this report for 3 units.

items: Brazos Financial NTLM hash extraction methodology
John the Ripper execution and results (Windows)
Hashcat execution and results (Windows)
Cracked passwords table with timestamps
Screenshots of Windows cracking process

sections.title: From Unit 2 (Lab 4.2 — Linux Password Cracking)
items: Alamo Industries SHA-512 hash extraction methodology
John the Ripper execution and results (Linux)
Hashcat execution and results (Linux)
Cracked passwords table with timestamps
Screenshots of Linux cracking process
Performance comparison notes (NTLM vs SHA-512)

sections.title: NEW This Unit
items: Part II: Cover Page, Executive Summary, Methodology
Part I: Timesheet with all 3 units, plus Analyst Reflection
Part II: Platform Comparison Analysis (NTLM vs SHA-512) as Findings
Part II: Client-Specific Recommendations (Brazos + Alamo)
Part II: Risk Analysis, Recommendations, Appendix with references

timeGuide.timesheetNote: This is Unit 3 of a 3-unit module — your first Engagement Packet with two clients. All time this unit is billable: split between Brazos Financial Group and Alamo Industries. Log as Client Billable: 3–4.5 hours — Password Security Assessment. You should already have 20 screenshots from Labs 4.1 and 4.2 in your Module4_Screenshots folder — verify the count before you begin.

tipCallout.title: You’re Already 60–70% Done
tipCallout.text: If you followed the Unit 1-2 instructions, most of your report is already written. This unit is about compilation and analysis, not starting over. Import your existing documentation, add the new analysis sections, and polish for submission.

content.title: Senior Penetration Tester, NEXUS Security
content.quote: We’ve got password assessments running for both Brazos and Alamo, and I’m putting you on both. Do the assessment the way you always do — full write-up, both platforms. This one stays internal, though: it comes to me, not the clients. I’ll pull what each client needs out of your report and repackage it into their own deliverable. So don’t burn time polishing a client-perfect executive summary — give me plenty to work with and let me do the trimming. Just don’t pad it: everything you hand me should be worth handing over.

content.whatYoureDoing: The foundation of your Engagement Packet — setting up the document and importing your work from Labs 4.1 and 4.2. This is an internal comparative engagement: you assess both clients’ platforms in one packet, and it comes to your senior analyst, not to the clients.
content.whyItMatters: Professional security reports follow consistent structures. This packet stays internal — Maya pulls from it to build each client’s separate deliverable — so your job is a complete, well-organized assessment of both platforms, cleanly labeled by client.

content.title: Include in Part I (Internal Packet)
items:
A. Timesheet (5%)
  • Hours per unit: Lab 4.1 (internal training), Lab 4.2 (internal training), Lab 4.3 (client billable to Brazos Financial and Alamo Industries)
  • Date, specific activity name, and hours per line entry (no “did lab” entries)
  • Internal vs. client-billable split clearly indicated
  • Justification narrative for any unusual entries (very high or very low hours on a task)
  • Total hours at the bottom

B. Runbook (20%)
  • One man-page-style entry per tool used across Labs 4.1, 4.2, and 4.3 (John the Ripper, Hashcat, etc.)
  • For each tool: name and purpose, switches used (with one-line explanations), switches not used but worth knowing
  • How you used the tool on the engagement (the actual command, including hash mode and attack mode)
  • What output came back (with screenshot reference, including cracked passwords or session statistics)
  • What you did with the result (how it fed into your platform comparison or Findings)
  • Bar: a colleague picking this up in 3 months should be able to reproduce your engagement

C. Analyst Reflection (15%)
  • Timesheet Self-Audit. Was there a task or section that took disproportionately long? Describe what made it slow (a hash mode that wouldn’t crack, attack mode selection, troubleshooting a Hashcat error) and what you’d do differently next time. Then total your hours: if Maya Rodriguez reviewed your timesheet alongside this deliverable, what conclusion would she draw about your efficiency?
  • Professional Reflection. What worked on this engagement? What didn’t? If you ran this password security assessment for Brazos and Alamo again next semester with what you know now, what would you do differently? Be specific — “I’d benchmark rules-based attacks before brute force” is more useful than “I’d be more organized.”
  • AI Methodology Reflection. Where AI helped you on this engagement, and how you validated what it told you. Strong answers describe specific moments of help and the verification step that followed (e.g., “I asked Claude to explain Hashcat mask syntax; I confirmed against the Hashcat documentation before writing my finding.”). “I didn’t use AI on this engagement (N/A)” is acceptable if true — but don’t claim N/A if your runbook is full of AI-generated explanations.
  • No word floor. No required structure. Honest reflection earns credit; filler doesn’t.

content.title: Include in Part II (Client Deliverable)
items:
Executive Summary (12%)
  • An internal handoff to your senior analyst, not a client-facing letter. Maya pulls from it to build each client’s separate report, so completeness beats polish — it’s fine to include more than any one client’s report would use.
  • Overall risk posture for each client, one line each (Brazos, Alamo)
  • Cracked-credential counts by platform (e.g., “Windows/NTLM: 47% — Linux/SHA-512: 12%”)
  • Key findings and recommendations, labeled by client so Maya can split them easily
  • Bullets are fine — no salutation, no “Dear client,” no first person
  • Err toward too much rather than too little — but no padding. Every item you include should be worthy of inclusion. Volume for its own sake helps no one; a senior analyst still has to read it.
  • No strict one-page limit this time — curation happens downstream. Make it dense and useful, not padded.

Methodology (7%)
  • 3–4 paragraph narrative of your engagement approach across both platforms
  • Phases of the engagement (e.g., hash extraction → identification → attack mode selection → cracking → cross-platform analysis)
  • Scope and limitations: what was in scope, what was not
  • Tools used at a high level (per-flag detail belongs in your Runbook, not here)
  • Written for the client, not for security peers — this is client-facing

Findings (22%)
  • Number each finding sequentially (Finding 1, Finding 2, ...)
  • Descriptive title (e.g., “Domain Administrators Using Easily-Cracked Passwords”)
  • Severity rating (Critical / High / Medium / Low / Informational)
  • Affected platform (Windows NTLM / Linux SHA-512) and client (Brazos / Alamo)
  • Description, including hash type, crack time, and attack method that succeeded
  • Evidence: reference to your screenshot(s) or session output (e.g., “See Figure 4 in Appendix”)
  • Impact statement in business terms (lateral movement risk, credential reuse, regulatory exposure)
  • Per-finding recommendation summary (one line — full remediation lives in Recommendations)
  • Order findings by severity, highest first

Risk Analysis (11%)
  • Per-finding likelihood × impact analysis
  • Business context per client: Brazos (financial services, regulatory exposure) vs. Alamo (manufacturing, IP and operational continuity)
  • Answer “so what?” for each finding — why does this matter to each business
  • Differentiated reasoning per finding (avoid uniform “high risk” labels with no logic)
  • Reference an authoritative risk framework where helpful (NIST SP 800-63B Digital Identity Guidelines, CIS Controls)

Recommendations (8%)
  • Prioritized: Monday-morning fixes (low effort, immediate impact) vs. next-quarter budget items (higher effort, strategic)
  • Each recommendation specific: what to do, what effort required, what priority window
  • Differentiated by client (some recs apply only to Brazos’s Windows environment, others to Alamo’s Linux environment)
  • Aligned with findings (each recommendation maps to one or more findings)
  • Where applicable, indicate which control framework the recommendation aligns with (NIST SP 800-63B, CIS Controls v8, NIST CSF)

content.text: How should organizations respond to a password breach? Research incident response procedures for compromised credentials.
NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2 - Computer Security Incident Handling Guide. Available at: https://csrc.nist.gov/publications/detail/sp/800-61/rev-2/final

items.label: Missing screenshots
items.text: Check your Lab4-Screenshots folder BEFORE starting. Count them.

items.label: Inconsistent headings
items.text: Use Word’s built-in heading styles for proper Table of Contents generation.

items.label: Copy/paste formatting
items.text: Use “Paste Special > Unformatted Text” to avoid carrying over weird formatting.

content.body: Chapter 16 covers security assessment methodology, including how penetration test findings are structured for reporting. Your Engagement Packet follows the same workflow Chapter 16 describes: document the tools used, record the methodology applied, and organize findings in a format the client can act on.

content.text: A Word document with all 12 section headers, imported content from Units 1-2, all 12 screenshots placed in appropriate sections, and placeholder text for the NEW sections you’ll complete in Checkpoints 2-4.

content.whatYoureDoing: The core analytical work of your report — comparing Windows NTLM and Linux SHA-512 password security architectures using your own cracking data.
content.whyItMatters: This is THE teaching moment of Module 4. You have empirical evidence showing that the same password cracks thousands of times faster on Windows than Linux. The difference isn’t password complexity — it’s defensive architecture. This is why security professionals argue about platform choices.

steps.text: Create a speed comparison table using your actual data:
Answer these four analysis questions in your report:
substeps: Why did NTLM hashes crack faster than SHA-512?
What defensive mechanisms does Linux use that Windows NTLM lacks?
Which client faces greater credential risk? Why?
What would you recommend each client prioritize?

steps.text: Explain the technical reasons for speed differences:
substeps: NTLM: Single-pass MD4, no salt, no iterations
SHA-512: Salted, 5000+ rounds of key derivation

content.text: Why does Windows still use NTLM? Research backward compatibility requirements and Active Directory authentication. Microsoft has been trying to deprecate NTLM for over a decade — why is it still everywhere?
If you research NTLM architecture, add it to your references: Microsoft Docs — NTLM Overview. Also add: Linux crypt(3) man page for SHA-512 implementation details.

items.label: Generic comparisons
items.text: Use YOUR actual H/s data, not hypotheticals.

items.label: Missing the “why”
items.text: Don’t just say “SHA-512 is slower” — explain salting and key stretching.

items.label: Forgetting business context
items.text: Connect technical findings to each client’s situation.

content.body: Chapter 15’s “Password Attacks” section covers brute force, dictionary attacks, and rainbow tables — the techniques you applied hands-on in Labs 4.1 and 4.2. Your platform comparison analysis translates Chapter 15’s theory into observed results: why NTLM without salting falls to rainbow tables while SHA-512 with per-user salts demands targeted, slower attacks.

content.text: A completed Platform Comparison section with a data table showing your actual H/s metrics, calculated differences (e.g., “NTLM cracked 8,500x faster”), and thoughtful answers to all four analysis questions with technical explanations.

content.whatYoureDoing: Tailored, prioritized remediation recommendations for both Brazos Financial and Alamo Industries based on your findings and their specific business contexts.
content.whyItMatters: Security findings without actionable recommendations are just bad news. Clients hire consultants for solutions, not just problems. Your recommendations need to be specific, prioritized, and consider each client’s unique constraints and objectives.

content.text: What is credential stuffing and how does it differ from password spraying? Research how attackers use breached credential databases.
OWASP Credential Stuffing Prevention Cheat Sheet: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Credential_Stuffing_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html

content.title: CTO, Brazos Financial Group
content.quote: I need ammunition for the board. They’ve been resistant to stricter password policies because ‘nothing has happened.’ Show me the data that proves our current approach is inadequate. Give me numbers I can put in a PowerPoint.

content.title: CEO, Alamo Industries
content.quote: We’re working toward CMMC certification for our DoD contracts. I need documentation showing we’re addressing credential security. Elena keeps telling me the shared supervisor account is a problem — Frank keeps blocking changes. Give me evidence to settle this.

substeps: Consider Active Directory migration path away from NTLM
Implement password complexity AND length requirements
Deploy MFA for all privileged accounts immediately
Establish password rotation policy with breach monitoring
Eliminate shared supervisor account (CMMC requirement)
Implement individual accountability for all access
Strengthen already-good SHA-512 implementation
Document controls for CMMC evidence package

steps.text: Prioritize each recommendation using High / Medium / Low based on three factors:
substeps: Risk severity — what happens if we don’t fix it?
Implementation complexity — how hard is this?
Business impact — does this affect operations?

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 15 covers password attack types including dictionary, hybrid, and OSINT-informed attacks. Your client-specific recommendations are grounded in the attack patterns described there. Chapter 16's coverage of security assessment methodology frames how your findings translate into prioritized, actionable guidance.

items.label: Generic recommendations
items.text: “Use stronger passwords” isn’t actionable. Be specific.

items.label: Ignoring client context
items.text: Brazos needs board ammunition; Alamo needs CMMC evidence.

items.label: Missing prioritization
items.text: Not everything is “Critical” — rank your recommendations.

content.text: Completed Risk Analysis and Recommendations sections with client-specific, prioritized remediation steps. Each recommendation should include what to do, why it matters, and priority level. Connect technical findings to each client’s specific business needs.

content.whatYoureDoing: The internal summary your senior analyst hands off from, plus your professional reflection and final quality checks.
content.whyItMatters: Maya builds each client’s report from your Executive Summary, so it needs to surface the key findings and recommendations clearly and completely — labeled by client. Give her plenty to work with; just make sure every point earns its place.

content.text: How do organizations enforce password policies at the system level? Research PAM (Pluggable Authentication Modules) configuration for password requirements.
The pam_pwquality(8) man page explains password quality requirements. Linux-PAM documentation: http://www.linux-pam.org/

steps.text: Write the Executive Summary (internal handoff — bullets are fine):
substeps: Engagement overview (who, what, why) — both clients
Key finding: platform architecture dramatically affects password security
Top risks per client, labeled Brazos / Alamo
Top recommendations per client, labeled Brazos / Alamo
No strict one-page limit — completeness over polish, but no padding: every point should be worth handing to Maya
What did you learn about password security that surprised you?
How would you approach a similar engagement differently?
What tools or techniques do you want to explore further?
Primary sources cited correctly (not the textbook — see Citations page)
NIST/OWASP/vendor documentation included
All URLs verified as accessible
Update Table of Contents
Check page numbers throughout
Grammar and spelling review
Screenshot quality and captions

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapters 15 and 16 together provide the technical foundation for your executive summary. Chapter 15 explains why NTLM is architecturally weak (no salt, no key stretching). Chapter 16 covers security assessment reporting standards — the professional communication skills you're practicing here mirror real-world penetration test deliverables.

items.label: Executive Summary too technical
items.text: Avoid jargon; write for non-technical readers.

items.label: Last-minute rushing
items.text: Budget time for quality checks; they matter.

items.label: Missing references
items.text: 10 minimum is required; this is a straightforward requirement to meet.

content.text: A complete, polished report with executive summary, professional reflection, 10+ references, and all 12 sections finalized. The document should be ready for submission with proper formatting, updated Table of Contents, and verified screenshot quality.

items.label: Target Prioritization
items.text: NTLM environments yield credentials faster — attack Windows AD before Linux systems.

items.label: OSINT Integration
items.text: Character dossiers demonstrate how social media reconnaissance improves password attacks.

items.label: Report Writing
items.text: Professional deliverables are part of the job — pentesters who can’t write don’t get promoted.

items.label: Architecture Matters
items.text: Password policy alone doesn’t fix NTLM — the hash algorithm is fundamentally weak.

items.label: Shared Accounts = No Accountability
items.text: The shift_supervisor problem exists everywhere in OT environments.

items.label: Defense in Depth
items.text: MFA, privileged access management, and credential monitoring compensate for weak hashes.

purpleTeam.connection: This module bridges attack and defense perspectives. You’ve used offensive tools (John the Ripper, Hashcat) to generate defensive intelligence (platform comparison, risk recommendations). This is purple team thinking: use attacker methodology to inform defender priorities.

sections.title: Report Sections (Part I + Part II)
items: Part I — Timesheet (hours by unit, internal vs. billable split)
Part I — Runbook (one entry per tool, reproducible)
Part I — Analyst Reflection (self-audit, professional, AI methodology)
Part II — Cover Page (title, clients, your name, date)
Part II — Executive Summary (internal handoff, labeled by client)
Part II — Methodology
Part II — Findings (labeled by client + platform, ordered by severity)
Part II — Risk Analysis (business impact per client)
Part II — Recommendations (prioritized, client-specific)
Part II — Appendix (raw outputs)
References (minimum 10)

sections.title: Documentation Verification
items: 20+ screenshots embedded across your Foundation Labs
VM hostname (kali-<your abc123>) visible in all terminal screenshots
All screenshots captioned appropriately
10+ valid references listed

sections.title: Submission Format
items: File format: .docx (NOT PDF)
Professional formatting throughout
Grammar and spelling checked

finalChecklist.intro: Verify all requirements before submitting to Canvas:
finalChecklist.canvasSubmission: Submit as .docx in Canvas before the deadline. PDF submissions are not accepted.

deadlineTip.title: Deadline Tip
deadlineTip.text: Submit at least 10 minutes before the deadline, even if it’s not perfect. Submissions are unlimited — you can keep uploading better versions until time runs out. A 70 beats a zero every time.

partII_client.note: Cover Page, Table of Contents, and Appendix are mechanical requirements graded via deductions, not the additive scale.

penalties.condition: Per screenshot missing or with VM hostname (kali-<your abc123>) not visible. Floor: 20 screenshots for Modules 1-4.
Per reference missing below the 10-reference minimum. References must meet the source quality standard.
Appendix missing from Part II Client Deliverable.
Table of Contents missing or hand-typed (must be auto-generated from heading styles).
Word fields not updated before submission (visible 'Error! Reference source not found' or stale figure numbers).

gradingStandards.rubricCallout: Full grading rubric and per-section level descriptors: Grading Info

bonus.title: Bonus Opportunity (+5%)
bonus.description: OSINT Wordlist: Research TV show characters to create a custom wordlist. Document your research methodology, sources used, and demonstrate improved crack rates against the character hashes.

gradingStandards.automaticZero: No submission, AI-generated content, fabricated screenshots, shared work, wrong format (PDF), or academic integrity violation.

channels.label: Discord
channels.detail: Post in #lab-help for technical issues (fastest response)

needHelp.lateWorkPolicy: Late submissions are not accepted. The course drop policy (1 lowest Engagement Packet dropped) exists for emergencies. This lab is eligible to be dropped, but incomplete or low-effort submissions should not be turned in with the intent to use the drop policy as a shield against academic standards.

permitted: Explain command syntax and tool functionality
Proofread and grammar check your writing
prohibited: Write entire sections of your report
genAI.principle: Think of AI like a tutor, not a ghostwriter. It can help you understand and improve, but the work itself must be yours.

whatsNext.title: Module 5: Infrastructure, Risk & Resilience
whatsNext.text: Next module you’ll work with LoneStar DevOps on vulnerability assessment and risk prioritization. Lab 5.1 introduces vulnerability scanning methodology. Start reviewing Chapter 10 (Infrastructure Security) and Chapter 20 (Risk Management) to prepare.
